preamble: The transformation of a voiced alveolar plosive /d/ into a voiced alveolo-palatal affricate /d͡ʑ/ is a common palatalization process in historical linguistics. This shift typically occurs when /d/ is followed by a high front vowel like /i/ or a palatal approximant /j/, where the tongue's articulation moves closer to the hard palate, resulting in both a palatalized articulation and an affricate release.

examples.note: The alveolar stop /d/ assibilated to a voiced alveolo-palatal affricate [d͡ʑ] before the high front vowel /i/.
Iotation, a type of palatalization, causes /d/ to shift to [d͡ʑ] when followed by the suffix -je.

sources: Radanović-Kocić, V. (2017). Suspended Morphology in Serbian: Clitics vs. Affixes. Glossa, 2(1).
